text: The surgeon demonstrates the dissection of an indirect inguinal hernia on the right side of a sixty-five-year-old male patient. The inferior epigastric vessels are visible, and the dissection begins at the medial umbilical ligament, progressing laterally. The preperitoneal plane appears thicker than usual, with fused layers. The surgeon moves laterally, buzzing on top of the fat and pushing it laterally over the nerves.

The surgeon attempts to re-establish the preperitoneal plane from the undersurface of the flap, but the tissue is too thin. The procedure continues medially into the supravesicular space, with the surgeon switching to an oblique angle and using electrocautery to buzz on top of the fat, moving towards the infra-upper gastric area.

The surgeon uses an oblique angle to apply cautery directly on the hernia sac, pushing surrounding fat away to expose the vas deferens. The deferential vessel, running medial to the vas, is preserved, contributing to the testicle's blood supply.

The surgeon gently pulls the hernia sac medially while using cautery to separate it from surrounding tissues. The spermatic cord is visible and well-preserved, with no cord lipoma present. The surgeon identifies the right iliac vein and proceeds with more confident medial dissection.
The surgeon performs sweeping motions to dissect around the hernia sac, revealing the iliopubic tract. The absence of a cord lipoma is noted.

The surgeon performs high lateral fixation using two ovicral sutures, followed by Cooper's fixation with the same suture technique. The internal oblique muscle is visible, and the undersurface is clearly exposed, allowing for a typical flap closure.
